item.title: An Arm Mainboard for the Framework Laptop
item.description: Using the repair-friendly Framework 13 laptop chassis, I've tested the low-end x86 option (a Ryzen AI 5 340 Mainboard), the fastest RISC-V option (DC-ROMA II), and today I'm publishing results from the only Arm Mainboard, the MetaComputing AI PC, which has a 12-core Arm SoC and up to 32 GB of soldered-on RAM. My Framework 13 has run on x86, RISC-V, and now Arm, making it something of a 'Ship of Theseus'.

item.title: Stop Flock
item.description: Flock Safety markets its devices as "AI-powered precision policing technology" - far beyond basic license plate readers (ALPRs) (Flock Safety). The system uses AI to create a "Vehicle Fingerprint" - identifying cars not only by license plate, but also by color, make and model, roof racks, dents/damage, wheel type, and more. Even bumper sticker placement is analyzed. This lets law enforcement search for a "blue sedan with damage on the left side" even without a license plate.

item.title: An exhaustive review of design tool hover areas - tldraw: Infinite Canvas SDK for React
item.description: The tldraw SDK provides tools, services, and APIs to build beautiful whiteboards and infinite canvas applications with real-time collaboration and a powerful React-based canvas.

item.title: How I run pi.dev safely (and actually get work done)
item.description: Most people are still using ChatGPT or Claude in a browser tab.

item.title: Saying Goodbye to Agile
item.description: Software's "Agile moment" has been and gone.

item.title: Moats Don't Work When Bridges Are Free
item.description: ## The prompt that changed my perspective I recently used Perplexity Computer to link my investment accounts together and build a portfolio tracker. Not a la...

item.title: If it could have, why didn't it? · Alex Gaynor
item.description: Mon, Apr 13, 2026

item.title: We are lucky to have LLMs as programmers
item.description: A (short) note from my blog. Read to gain insights. Or don't, lol.

item.title: has_not_been_viewed_much — Will W.
item.description: Museum APIs and the art that hasn't been viewed much.

item.title: When You Can't Fix the Database, You Contain It
item.description: How we introduced an anti-corruption layer over a legacy database without breaking the rest of the system

item.title: Your codebase doesn't care how it got written
item.description:
[10 min read] Every design team I’ve worked with has had the same complaint about clients.
“They sent over a PowerPoint with mockups.” Eye rolls. Crossed a...
item.author: (Robby Russell)

item.title: How can I keep from singing?
item.description: This is a story of a most unexpected journey — one that is just starting. If somebody had told me four years ago that I’d be writing this today, I would have stared at them in astonishment or laughed dismissedly. Yet here I am, writing about a late-found hobby that has somehow become a very important part of my life.

item.title: Why My WordPress? – Alex Kirk
item.description: Most WordPress plugins are built to grow your audience. I’ve been going in the opposite direction for years: building tools not to increase reach, but for personal reasons: to stay connected to people I care about, to keep memories that would otherwise scatter, to own the data that documents my own life.

item.title: New Twin Dad — Edward Champion
item.description: Being a new dad is so hard. Things change drastically from day to day, you're getting such broken sleep, and you have a whole new person to love. Being a twin dad is like x2. So I wanted to offer what little advice and reassurance I can, and hope that this helps even one new person. If you're a new dad who is struggling (I nearly had a breakdown on day 2!), please feel free to reach out. I'm always here to support someone tackling the hardest thing they'll ever do.

item.title: How We Build Product Teams at Owner.com | Dean Bloembergen
item.description: How Owner.com reached $15M ARR with 5 engineers. A blueprint for building small, elite product teams by Dean Bloembergen, Co-founder & CTO of Owner.com.

item.title: Sam Baumann
item.description: I created an automation that pulls my highlights from kindle and drops them into my obsidian vault.

item.title: Completion is a Substrate, not a UI
item.description: ICR is not a convenience feature. It is a structural change in how the cost of an interaction scales with the size of the underlying data.
item.author: (Charlie Holland)

item.title: SVG based Vector Scope
item.description: I was gifted a Sencore SC62 oscilloscope from the mid-80s. It had spent a good chunk of its 40+ year life in a barn. This beautiful device along with many similar models are being discarded since modern scopes have become so cheap and capable. To avoid that fate I wanted to repurpose it into something that would allow it to live indoors once again.

item.title: The Vibes Don't Scale
item.description: Vibe coding works great for the first few PRs. Then you do it two hundred more times and the codebase quietly stops making sense. The fix isn't better prompts — it's building a machine that compounds.

item.title: Building a Grow-Only Counter on a Sequentially Consistent KV Store
item.description: Exploring Fly.io's Grow-Only Counter challenge: CAS-based and CRDT-like solutions on a sequentially consistent KV store, and what sequential consistency actually guarantees.

item.title: Absurd In Production
item.description: Five months of durable execution with just Postgres.
item.author: (Armin Ronacher)

item.title: A backend where you never need migrations or auth code
item.description: The story behind MonsterWriter and LinkedRecords — a 8-year side project journey from Google Docs envy to building a generic backend for SPAs.

item.title: How I made my skills update themselves · Joost.blog
item.description: Skills install as loose folders with no package manager. Here's a pattern that makes each skill check for updates — and install them — when you invoke it.
item.author: (https://joost.blog/about-me/)

item.title: Please don't vibe code your personal website
item.description: I have experimented enough with AI coding agents that I have come to pick up on their design style, and I've been noticing it on the internet more and more. Some of its stylistic choices I can articulate: too much animation, pointless elements, too many colors, sloppy overuse of haptics, buttons that are too padded, and interfaces that are simultaneously too complex and too simple. But there is also just a feel to these websites. When I see a personal website that is vibe coded, I can immediately tell, and I am immediately disappointed.

item.title: Automate work with routines - Claude Code Docs
item.description: Put Claude Code on autopilot. Define routines that run on a schedule, trigger on API calls, or react to GitHub events from Anthropic-managed cloud infrastructure.

item.title: Internet será irrespirable los días de fútbol y otros deportes. Telefónica extiende los bloqueos a Champions, tenis y golf.
Getting the Flock out
item.description: I wrote to Flock’s privacy contact to opt out of their …

item.title: Terminator: The Story of the 6502 Code You See Onscreen
item.description:
What are the origins of the 6502 assembly code that you see onscreen through the Terminator's vision? Dave traces its origins and explains its purpose.
Comments with question marks get answered on our weekly ShopTalk!: https://youtu.be/iT0T52OV9B8
item.author: (Dave's Garage)

item.title: Thousands of rare concert recordings are landing on the Internet Archive -- listen now | TechCrunch
item.description: Chicago-based music superfan Aadam Jacobs has been recording the concerts he goes to since the 1980s, amassing an archive of over 10,000 tapes.
